verses.text: A aliança de Josafá com Acabe levou-o a comprometer sua fidelidade ao Senhor (veja **19.2-3** ; cp. **2Co 6.14-18** ). O casamento de seu filho Jeorão ( **2Cr 21.1** ) com a filha de Acabe, Atalia ( **22.2** ), trouxe a apostasia de Israel para Judá (veja **21.5-6** , **12-13** ; **22.1-4** ).

A aliança de Josafá com Acabe levou-o a comprometer sua fidelidade ao Senhor (veja **19.2-3** ; cp. **2Co 6.14-18** ). O casamento de seu filho Jeorão ( **2Cr 21.1** ) com a filha de Acabe, Atalia ( **22.2** ), trouxe a apostasia de Israel para Judá (veja **21.5-6** , **12-13** ; **22.1-4** ).
18:3 To Ramoth-gilead - This place belonged to the Israelites, and was now held by the king of Syria. The whole of this chapter is circumstantially explained in the notes on 1 Kings 22:1-53.

18:4 This is almost word for word the same with what we had, 1 Kings 22. We will not repeat what was there said, nor have we much to add, but may take occasion to think, 1. Of the great duty of acknowledging God in all our ways and enquiring at his word, whatever we undertake. Jehoshaphat was not willing to proceed till he had done this, 2 Chr 18:4. By particular believing prayer, by an unbiased consultation of the scripture and our own consciences, and by an observant regard to the hints of providence, we may make such enquiries and very much to our satisfaction.

Os 400 profetas eram os "bajuladores" (sim-senhores) de Acabe. Josafá queria ouvir, em vez disso, um profeta do Senhor, mas Acabe sabia de antemão que tal profeta iria contradizer seus profetas da corte.

Os 400 profetas eram os "bajuladores" (sim-senhores) de Acabe. Josafá queria ouvir, em vez disso, um profeta do Senhor, mas Acabe sabia de antemão que tal profeta iria contradizer seus profetas da corte.

18:8 The king of Israel called for one of his officers, and said, Fetch quickly Micaiah: Ahab summons the prophet whose oracle he does not want (18:7). The naming of the messenger — and the haste — set up the tension. Truth-tellers are recalled only when court-prophets have already filled the room with comforting lies.

18:9 The king of Israel and Jehoshaphat - "Ahab consulted false prophets; but Jehoshaphat sought instruction from the presence of the Lord, and prayed at the entering in of Samaria; and before these all the false prophets prophesied lies." - Targum.

18:10 Zedekiah the son of Chenaanah had made him horns of iron: A prophetic act-symbol — iron horns to figure Israel pushing Syria back. The visual aid amplifies a false oracle (cp. Jer 23:30-32). Even effective theater cannot give the spirit of true prophecy; the spectacle's confidence outran its truth.

18:11 All the prophets prophesied so: The court chorus reinforces Zedekiah's word (18:10). Numbers do not establish truth — four hundred against one (18:7) was the wrong arithmetic. The pattern recurs in Israel: the prophet of God often stood alone (cp. 1 Kgs 18:22; Jer 26:7-19).

18:12 The messenger spake to him, saying, Behold, the words of the prophets declare good: The messenger urges Micaiah toward conformity with the court consensus. The pressure is sociological, not theological — *davar tov* (a good word) is what the king wants to hear. The pressure to flatter is one of prophecy's oldest temptations (cp. 2 Tim 4:3).

18:13 As the Lord liveth, even what my God saith, that will I speak: Micaiah's standing answer to the messenger. *Hai-YHWH* — the oath formula — binds his speech to God's word alone. The verse is the prophetic charter: a true messenger has surrendered the right to choose his content (cp. Num 22:18; Jer 23:28).

18:14 Go ye up, and prosper: Micaiah at first parrots the false oracle ironically. The king himself recognizes the mockery and adjures him to speak truth (18:15) — a tacit admission that Ahab knew all along he was being told what he wanted to hear, and only now demanded the real word.

Acabe percebeu que Micaías não havia falado a palavra do Senhor, talvez porque a resposta de Micaías usou as mesmas palavras que a de seus rivais, ou Micaías poderia estar falando com um tom sarcástico ( **18.14** ).

18:16 I did see all Israel scattered upon the mountains, as sheep that have no shepherd: Micaiah's true vision — Israel without a king after the battle. The image of shepherdless sheep recurs (cp. Num 27:17; Ezek 34:5; Matt 9:36), naming a community without provision and protection. The oracle predicts Ahab's death (18:34).

18:17 Did I not tell thee that he would not prophesy good unto me, but evil?: Ahab confirms to Jehoshaphat that he had expected the unwelcome word. The verse exposes the king's double mind — he had hoped to hear comfort, while knowing comfort was unwarranted. The willful blindness will not save him from the truth's outcome.
A visão esclarece o engano dos falsos profetas enquanto legitima a palavra de Micaías. O engano assegurou que Acabe cairia em batalha; seu julgamento foi decretado.\n\n

18:23 Zedekiah the son of Chenaanah came near, and smote Micaiah upon the cheek: The false prophet strikes the true. Physical violence against the truth-teller foreshadows the suffering pattern of all faithful prophecy — culminating in the Lord himself struck before the Sanhedrin (Mic 5:1; John 18:22; Acts 23:2).

18:24 Behold, thou shalt see on that day when thou shalt go into an inner chamber to hide thyself: Micaiah's reply — the day will come when Zedekiah hides for fear. Vindication awaits the prophet of the Lord; the false prophet who flourishes in public will end in private terror (cp. Jer 28:15-17).
A rebeldia de Acabe contra o Senhor é evidenciada por seu desprezo por Micaías, o profeta do Senhor.
A rebeldia de Acabe contra o Senhor é evidenciada por seu desprezo por Micaías, o profeta do Senhor.

18:27 If thou certainly return in peace, then hath not the Lord spoken by me: Micaiah stakes his prophetic legitimacy on the outcome — the Deuteronomic test of prophecy (Deut 18:22). Ahab does not return; the test resolves in Micaiah's favor (18:33-34), though the king is too dead to recognize the verdict.

Embora Acabe soubesse que havia agido contra a vontade de Deus e, portanto, era responsável pelas consequências, sua preocupação imediata era com o exército arameu. Sua crença de que poderia desafiar o julgamento de Deus contra ele indica seu desrespeito pelo Senhor; seu erro foi fatal.\n\n
